I had never heard of this woman before but just started watching this, 1 episode in.  People might not believe this but it's 100% true.  In the summer of '99 my family took a cruise, it was on the Rhapsody of the Seas, in Alaska. Our 2nd or 3rd night my cousin and I stayed out later and went to the club, we were going on the side where there was a little balcony and several members of the ban were standing around, immediately I noticed the smell of pot, we walked over and I asked if they knew were we might be able to get a little.  One of them glanced around then handed me a little bag and said, it's on the house my sea fearing brother, I said thanks and then we stood around about 15 minutes and passed around a joint and talked with them, when they were heading back in to the club, we went to walk off and one of the other guys ran over to us and said, hey man, you want any powder, I said nah we're good with a little smoke but thanks. He turned around and walked back in.  I looked at pictures after watching and reading about this case and that guy looked very VERY similar to that Alister Douglas guy.
I don't know if that guy still worked for the cruise in '99 and if the crew is different or rotates since this cruise was in Alaska and not tropical, but I'm like 90% sure it was the same guy who tried to sell us coke that night.

reply_text: There's something called international maritime. Our laws here in the states really don't apply once you're in international waters or somewhere we have no jurisdiction. You legally can't just subpoena someone's ip address based off of an Internet hunch. Granted, it did show location as Barbados but again, that's not probable cause nor would it matter even if it was, considering it's not the United States. And the parents and brother did go back to look. If she did end up smuggled and essentially loaned out as a prost, they'd be island hopping her. So someone could see her at X one day. Then the next she could end up in a completely different geographical location. Now if something takes place in International waters like say a ship sinking or an accident on the water.. whoever's flag is being flown on the vessel gets what is essentially custody or jurisdiction to run their investigation but this only applies to the actual ship. Not the surrounding land or country.. which is where everyone thinks she was.

video_title: Amy Bradley Is Missing | Official Trailer | Netflix
reply_text: ​@Lucija-o7qI work in victim services. We call sightings like this sympathetic sightings. It's where people see something that makes them uncomfortable. Like get a strange look from someone. Or hear a conversation or have an interaction that makes them uncomfortable. So they probably have stumbled on something that's unusual or criminal in nature. Then their mind constantly holds on to that uncomfortable feeling. And you see the news and you see a situation and it kind of fits. And you call in because you've convinced yourself. This is what you saw. It happens all the time especially with missing people cases. We had a case where I'm from and a family I worked with. Their child went missing 5 years ago. The family was getting reports of sightings for the whole 5 years. Quite recently they found the child's body. They found the person who did it and the child's been dead. Probably 24 hours after they were taken. All of those people who called the ass. Some of them just want to be assholes but most of them are just good people trying to do good things. People that who think they're doing the right thing. The only one that leaves me questioning it a little bit is the naval officer. If he is being truthful and not just making stuff up for his 5 minutes which from his personality seems totally something he would do. But on the chance that he's being truthful, I don't know. That's the only one. That makes me question. Plus passing a polygraph test is pseudoscience at best.
